narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like academic records, test scores, and background to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 3.00, LSAT score of 25.50, upper-middle family income, full-time student status, and notably low Year 1 and Year 3 school decile rankings of 1—and predicted a 90.9% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This high failure probability directly led to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar to maintain their strong pass rates. The three most influential factors in this decision were your Year 3 decile ranking, your LSAT score, and your Year 1 decile ranking, all of which strongly pushed the prediction toward failure rather than success. Your Year 3 decile of 1, well below the typical student's average of around 5.5, had the biggest negative impact, signaling to the model that your later academic performance placed you in the bottom tier compared to peers who generally pass the bar. Similarly, your LSAT score of 25.50—much lower than the dataset average of 36.70—reinforced concerns about your readiness for the rigors of law school and the bar. Even your Year 1 decile of 1, again far below the average of about 5.7, contributed by highlighting early struggles that often predict ongoing challenges. While your upper-middle income and full-time status are common among admitted students and didn't sway things much, they couldn't offset these core academic red flags. In comparison to typical students who pass, your rankings and LSAT put you in the lowest percentiles, where bar failure rates are unfortunately high. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background, but a data-driven assessment based on patterns from past students. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score significantly, strengthening your grades to climb those school deciles, and perhaps seeking targeted prep courses or academic advising tailored for Asian male applicants facing similar hurdles. With focused improvements, you could reapply stronger next cycle and shift the model's view in your favor.
